feed.title: Combined summary - Quantum Sunset Economics: a working paper analyzing PACT adoption

entry.title: Combined summary - Quantum Sunset Economics: a working paper analyzing PACT adoption
entry.summary: Ahmed Raza, an independent researcher, recently published a working paper that provides a detailed analysis of Dan Robinson's PACTs proposal. The paper includes a comparative framework across various policy options and a regulatory analysis concerning CRQC-arrival scenarios. It also features a Python reference implementation of the commitment side of the protocol, which can be accessed [here](https://github.com/ahmedrazaumer1/pact-commit). One of the notable findings from Raza's research is that the exposed-key UTXO surface accounts for approximately 25% of the circulating Bitcoin supply, which equates to around five million coins including the 1.1M Patoshi cluster. This was determined through a BigQuery cohort analysis provided in the supplementary materials. Raza's analysis suggests that under any scenario where privacy costs are convex, the largest dormant holders are the least likely to engage voluntarily with the system—a characteristic that is inherent to any voluntary mechanism rather than a specific flaw in Robinson's design. He proposes that early standardization, coupled with a pooling service that evens out privacy costs across holder sizes, could be a critical step forward. In this model, a solo holder of 100k BTC would face significantly reduced privacy costs compared to the current model. The paper also raises questions about the assumptions used in the analysis, particularly the convexity assumption in its economic model and the practical implementation of the proposed pooling service, whether it should be custodial, non-custodial, or multisig-based. Another discussion point is whether P2TR should be classified similarly to P2PK in terms of exposure risk. In another part of the correspondence, concerns were raised regarding the classification of quantum-exposed UTXOs, specifically addressing the complexities of script types like p2sh and their risk profiles. The discussion also touched on the potential security vulnerabilities posed by xpubs, noting that while they represent a significant vector of attack, quantifying the exact risk is challenging. The dialogue highlights the intricacies of blockchain privacy and the sophisticated nature of managing and mitigating risks in cryptocurrency systems.
